one_line_takeaway: Jointly optimizing text (GCG) and audio (PGD) adversarial perturbations against Spoken Language Models yields jailbreak rates up to 20x higher than either modality attacked alone, and a cheaper sequential approximation (SAMA) recovers most of this gain at 4x-6x lower compute cost.
key_finding: JAMA outperforms both unimodal baselines (GCG-only and PGD-only, with the other modality fixed/absent) by 1.5x to 20x in jailbreak success rate across the four SLMs and four audio types. Concretely, GCG-only already achieves high rates on Qwen2.5 Omni and Qwen2 Audio at suffix length 16 (59% and 90.9% respectively in one reported grid), while Gemma 3N resists GCG-only even at 4x longer suffixes (as low as 3.0%). PGD-only is consistently weaker than GCG-only, attributed to the speech encoder dampening perturbations; music is the best PGD base audio, while audiobook/conversational-speech semantic content appears to interfere with attack success. Where either unimodal attack already saturates (e.g., Qwen2 Audio + music), JAMA acts mainly as an amplifier; where both unimodal attacks are individually weak (e.g., intermediate GCG lengths of 4-8), JAMA shows a clear interaction/synergy effect, and this is most pronounced for Gemma 3N, which is robust to GCG-only and only weakly affected by PGD-only but becomes vulnerable under JAMA with longer PGD segments (especially with music initialization). Gradient-energy analysis shows GCG dominates optimization early, PGD dominates later, motivating SAMA. SAMA closes to within about a 10 percentage-point average jailbreak-rate gap of JAMA (narrowing further as GCG/PGD lengths increase, and occasionally SAMA outperforms JAMA, attributed to avoiding opposing gradient interference), while requiring 4x-6x less compute time than JAMA (measured on a single H100 GPU, 8 GCG tokens/4s PGD configuration) because it avoids conditioning every GCG candidate evaluation on the adversarial audio. Embedding analysis: a linear classifier on PCA-reduced last-layer embeddings distinguishes benign/GCG-only/PGD-only/JAMA conditions with up to 99% accuracy using only the first two principal components, and PGD-only centroid distance from the benign cluster is consistently larger than GCG-only's, despite PGD-only being the weaker attack in raw success rate.
core_contribution: The paper introduces JAMA (Joint Audio-text Multimodal Attack), a white-box jailbreak method for Spoken Language Models (SLMs) that simultaneously optimizes a discrete adversarial text suffix via Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) and a continuous adversarial audio perturbation via Projected Gradient Descent (PGD), jointly minimizing a shared loss over an affirmative target response averaged across a batch of malicious queries (Algorithm 1). This is presented as the first gradient-based attack to optimize both text and audio modalities of an SLM at once, rather than optimizing one modality while leaving the other fixed/unoptimized as prior unimodal attacks do. The authors also propose SAMA (Sequential Audio-text Multimodal Attack), which approximates JAMA by first running GCG to convergence without any audio input, then fixing the resulting suffix and optimizing the PGD audio perturbation conditioned on it -- motivated by an empirical observation that in JAMA the GCG gradient dominates early in training and its relative contribution diminishes later (gradient-energy-ratio analysis).
threat_model: White-box, gradient-based adversary with full access to model weights and a differentiable audio feature extractor. The attacker crafts, per malicious query, an adversarial text suffix appended to the prompt (GCG, Top-K candidate substitution search, search width 32, top-k 16, special tokens masked, 1000 steps) and an L2-norm-bounded imperceptible perturbation added to a base audio signal (PGD, learning rate 0.01, clip epsilon 0.001, 1000 steps), both optimized jointly against a frozen target SLM to elicit an affirmative ('Sure, here is...') response to a harmful request. The authors note raw waveforms are not directly differentiable in most SLMs (numpy-based or gradient-shattering feature extractors) and had to rewrite feature-extractor numpy operations into torch to enable backpropagation, restricting model selection to those where this was feasible.
limitations: The paper's own text notes: (1) the joint attack requires white-box gradient access and a differentiable audio feature extractor, which restricted model selection to those without gradient-shattering or non-differentiable (numpy) front-ends, meaning findings may not generalize to models/pipelines that resist gradient flow into raw audio; (2) SAMA occasionally outperforms JAMA in ways the authors say they cannot fully explain, attributing it to unspecified 'gradient interaction effects' (e.g., opposing GCG/PGD gradients) and explicitly leaving this open to future work; (3) Gemma 3N with Switchboard-initialized PGD is called out as an outlier where SAMA beats JAMA by ~20 percentage points under optimal conditions, again unexplained; (4) t-SNE/embedding analysis could separate attack-component subspaces but could not cleanly separate successful vs. unsuccessful JAMA jailbreaks, which the authors hypothesize lies in a non-linear space they did not further characterize; (5) results depend on the specific AdvBench-derived train/test split (only 8 training examples) and five seeds, plus a relatively narrow set of four models and four base audios.

datasets_used: AdvBench (harmful behaviors) - 8 training samples from first 40 of train split; evaluated on remaining 480 test samples
Switchboard corpus (two conversational speech samples used as PGD base audio, one male one female)
Audiobook reading (read speech, male) used as PGD base audio
Music performance (non-speech) used as PGD base audio
quantitative_results: JAMA surpasses unimodal jailbreak rate by 1.5x to 20x across four SLMs and four audio types
GCG-only baseline at suffix length 16: 59% (Qwen2.5 Omni) and 90.9% (Qwen2 Audio) jailbreak rate in reported grid
Gemma 3N resists GCG-only even at 4x longer suffixes: jailbreak rate as low as 3.0%
SAMA vs JAMA jailbreak-rate gap averages ~10 percentage points, narrowing as GCG/PGD lengths increase; SAMA sometimes outperforms JAMA
SAMA requires 4x-6x less compute time than JAMA (measured on single NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU, config: 8 GCG tokens, 4-second PGD audio)
Linear classifier on PCA-reduced embeddings distinguishes benign/GCG-only/PGD-only/JAMA conditions with up to 99% accuracy using first two principal components
Gemma 3N + Switchboard PGD init: SAMA outperforms JAMA by ~20 percentage points under optimal conditions (noted outlier)
baselines_compared: PGD-only attack (text suffix S = empty set, audio perturbation delta optimized) [following Gupta et al., 'I am bad']
GCG-only attack (audio perturbation x = 0, text suffix S optimized) [Zou et al., 'Universal and Transferable Adversarial Attacks on Aligned Language Models']
No-attack baseline (S = empty set, x = 0), marked as the (0,0) grid cell
